questions.question: Explain the algorithm to merge two sorted singly linked lists in O(m + n) time. Include the edge cases you must handle.
keyPoints: Initialize result list with dummy header for simplification
Compare nodes from both lists simultaneously
Attach smaller node to result, advance that list's pointer
Handle remaining nodes when one list exhausts first
Edge cases: empty lists, lists with single node, all elements of one list smaller

questions.question: Derive the time and space complexity for reversing a singly linked list using: (a) iterative approach (b) recursive approach
keyPoints: Iterative: O(n) time, O(1) space - single traversal with 3 pointers
Recursive: O(n) time due to call stack, O(n) space for recursion depth
Proof: recurrence T(n) = T(n-1) + O(1) solves to O(n)
Iterative preferred for embedded systems due to stack limitations
Recursive cleaner but risks stack overflow for large lists

questions.question: Describe how linked lists can represent polynomials. Explain the algorithm for adding two polynomials with different exponents.
keyPoints: Each node stores coefficient and exponent, ordered by descending exponent
Addition algorithm: traverse both lists simultaneously
If exponents match: add coefficients, create new node if sum non-zero
If one exponent larger: copy that term to result
Continue until both lists exhausted, handle remaining terms
Time complexity: O(m + n) where m, n are polynomial term counts

questions.question: What is a sparse matrix? Design a linked representation for sparse matrices and analyze its space complexity.
keyPoints: Sparse matrix: majority elements are zero (typically >90%)
Orthogonal list representation: header nodes for each row and column
Non-zero elements stored in node with row, col, value, row-link, col-link
Space: O(m + n + nz) for headers + O(5nz) for non-zero nodes
Comparison: 2D array uses O(mn), linked uses O(m + n + nz)
Significant savings when nz << mn

questions.question: Explain Floyd's cycle detection algorithm. Provide mathematical proof that the algorithm always detects a cycle if one exists.
keyPoints: Use slow pointer (1 step) and fast pointer (2 steps)
If cycle exists, they must meet inside cycle
Proof: When slow enters cycle at distance d from meeting point, fast is ahead
Relative speed = 1 step/iteration, fast closes gap by 1 each iteration
Maximum iterations to meet ≤ cycle length (number of nodes in cycle)
If fast reaches null, no cycle exists

questions.question: Compare singly and doubly linked lists in terms of operations they efficiently support. When would you choose one over the other?
keyPoints: Singly: O(1) insertion at head, O(n) deletion at tail (no prev pointer)
Doubly: O(1) deletion given node pointer, O(1) traversal backward
Memory: Doubly uses 2 pointers vs 1 - 50% more per node
Choose singly when: memory critical, only forward traversal needed
Choose doubly when: frequent deletions, need to traverse backward, implement undo operations

questions.question: Design an algorithm to split a circular linked list into two circular linked lists at a given position k. Analyze all edge cases and provide time complexity.
keyPoints: Handle k=0: first list empty, second is original
Handle k≥length: first is original, second is empty
General case: traverse to k-th node, break links to create two cycles
Need to find tail (last node before k) and k-th node
Tail's next becomes k-th node; k-th node's next becomes old head
Time: O(n) to find position, O(k) to split - overall O(n)
Space: O(1) - only pointer manipulation

questions.question: Explain the header node (sentinel) technique in linked lists. Show how it simplifies insertion and deletion operations with examples. What is the trade-off?
keyPoints: Header node is dummy node at position 0, contains no data
Insertion at position 0: no special case, same as any position
Deletion of first node: no null-check needed
Empty list: header exists, next is null - no special empty-handling code
Trade-off: one extra node per list, small memory overhead
Code simplification: uniform algorithm for all positions
Prove: eliminates 2+ special cases in typical implementations

questions.question: Given two polynomials represented as linked lists, design an efficient algorithm to multiply them. Analyze the time complexity and discuss optimizations.
keyPoints: Naive: multiply each term of P1 by all terms of P2 = O(m × n)
Result degree = degree(P1) + degree(P2), may have up to m+n-1 terms
For dense result: create array of size max_degree+1 for coefficients
Then build linked list from array - O(m + n + D) where D is result degree
Optimization: if result sparse (many coefficients zero), use hash-based accumulation
Space trade-off: array method uses O(D) extra space
